metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope/What section that 'MCP facade' is covered by calling `rebar._reads.*` (or `rebar.show_ticket()`) in-process, since `_mcp_reads.py` delegates directly to the library path with no separate freshness seam.
findings.finding: The plan treats CLI, library, and MCP as three independently-testable facade entries, but MCP has no distinct entry point: MCP read tools in `_mcp_reads.py` call `rebar.show_ticket()` → `rebar._reads.show_ticket()` → `ticket_reads.ensure_fresh()`, the identical in-process path as the library facade. There is no separate MCP-specific hook to exercise.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'after marker expiry each of CLI, library, MCP sees it' and Scope: 'CLI/library/MCP entry'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-AC proving description for AC2 that requires an explicit assertion on the fetch call count (e.g. `assert fetch_counter.call_count == 1`) after N reads within the throttle window, so the cardinality is mechanically enforced.
findings.finding: No per-AC proving command is specified beyond the catch-all 'make test green'; AC2 in particular requires a cardinality assertion ('exactly one fetch total') that 'make test' cannot demonstrate without an explicit count-check in the test code, and no such assertion pattern is described.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2: 'Repeated reads within the throttle window produce exactly one fetch total.' and AC5: 'make test green.'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2 add: 'Verified by asserting `mock_fetch.call_count == 1` after three back-to-back reads through all three facades within a single throttle window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan's mechanism description from 'ticket-sync.sh _reconverge_tickets' to 'rebar._store.sync.reconverge() (the in-process port; ticket-sync.sh was retired in Tier D)' to match the actual call site the new counted-fetch seam must intercept.
findings.finding: The plan (and the ensure_fresh docstring it paraphrases) asserts the mechanism reused is '_reconverge_tickets function in ticket-sync.sh', but reads.py lines 238-239 explicitly state 'Tier D retired the bash helper; rebar._store.sync is the sole impl' — the actual reconverge call is rebar._store.sync.reconverge(). The plan's premise about what shared mechanism the new tests must exercise is based on a stale docstring.
findings.location: Why section — 'one reconvergence impl shared by CLI/library/MCP; remote pushes visible within <=1 min; one throttle marker with no double-fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section, replace the parenthetical about ticket-sync.sh with: 'rebar._store.sync.reconverge() — the in-process port of the former bash helper, called from ensure_fresh in reads.py'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace vague terms in AC3 and AC4 with specific observable assertions: for AC3 state what value/type local replay must return (e.g. 'returns the last cached value unchanged'); for AC4 state the concrete post-failure check (e.g. 'returns cached state and completes within 2 s with no exception raised').
findings.finding: AC3 'local replay still works' and AC4 'consistent local state (no stall/corruption)' use vague outcome terms that do not specify the observable assertion — an executor cannot write a deterministic test without knowing what 'works' and 'consistent' mean concretely.
findings.suggested_fix: AC3: 'Each opt-out keeps the remote update invisible (read returns the pre-push cached value) while local replay returns data without raising.' AC4: 'A simulated fetch failure causes each facade to return the last cached local value and complete within 2 s with no exception propagated to the caller.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fetch-seam mechanism: state the exact patch target (e.g. `unittest.mock.patch('rebar.reads._do_fetch', wraps=original)` with a call counter) and confirm it intercepts every facade's fetch path.
findings.finding: The 'counted fetch seam' mechanism is unspecified — an executor must independently design whether this is a monkey-patch on reads.py, a fixture-injected counter wrapper, a subprocess call counter, or something else; two different implementers will produce incompatible designs.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New test(s) over reads.py ensure_fresh + the throttle marker + CLI/library/MCP entry' and 'two-clone parity test with a counted fetch seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'The fetch seam is implemented as `unittest.mock.patch("rebar.reads._do_fetch", wraps=_do_fetch)` with a `side_effect` counter; all three facades must route through this single symbol so the call count is authoritative.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the marker-expiry mechanism the test must use (e.g. 'delete the throttle marker file directly' or 'freeze time with `freezegun` to advance past the TTL') and confirm the marker file path is obtained programmatically from the same constant used by production code.
findings.finding: AC1 names 'marker expiry' as a precondition for remote visibility, but no step or fixture description explains how the test engineers marker expiry — sleep, mtime backdating, marker deletion, or a time-freeze — leaving an executor to invent a potentially flaky mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1: 'Push a remote update; after marker expiry each of CLI, library, MCP sees it.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or a new 'Test setup' section: 'Marker expiry is simulated by deleting `reads.THROTTLE_MARKER_PATH` (imported from production code) directly in the test fixture teardown/setup, avoiding any real sleep.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the fetch-failure scenario to the What or Scope section so AC4 has a corresponding described deliverable (e.g., 'inject a fetch error via the counted fetch seam and assert each facade returns consistent local state without stall or corruption').
findings.finding: AC4 ('A fetch failure yields consistent local state in each facade') has no corresponding described work in the What or Scope sections — it is an orphan criterion with no deliverable backing it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 vs. What / Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, extend the description: 'Add a two-clone parity test with a counted fetch seam exercising all three facades, including a fetch-failure injection scenario that asserts consistent local-state fallback (no stall/corruption) in CLI, library, and MCP.' Mirror this in Scope with an explicit bullet: '- Fetch-failure injection test using the counted fetch seam.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology by adding a parenthetical in the Why section that equates 'reconvergence impl' with 'reads.py::ensure_fresh', e.g. 'one reconvergence impl (reads.py::ensure_fresh) shared by CLI/library/MCP'.
findings.finding: The plan uses two different names for the same concept: 'reconvergence impl' (Why) and 'ensure_fresh' (Scope/reads.py), with no explicit bridge between them, creating potential confusion for an implementer unfamiliar with the codebase.
findings.location: Why section ('one reconvergence impl shared by CLI/library/MCP') vs Scope section ('reads.py ensure_fresh')
findings.suggested_fix: In Why, change 'one reconvergence impl shared by CLI/library/MCP' to 'one reconvergence impl (reads.py::ensure_fresh) shared by CLI/library/MCP'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the 'counted fetch seam' mechanism (monkeypatch, DI hook, subprocess wrapper, etc.) and, if it requires a production-code change in `reads.py`, acknowledge and justify that coupling in the plan.
findings.finding: The 'counted fetch seam' mechanism is unspecified: if making fetches countable/interceptable requires modifying production code in `reads.py` (e.g., adding an injectable hook or callback), that is an unacknowledged cross-component dependency introduced silently by the test plan.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 2 ('Repeated reads within the throttle window produce exactly one fetch total')
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer a monkeypatch/unittest.mock approach at the `reads.py` module boundary (e.g., patch `reads._do_fetch` or equivalent) so no production code changes are needed; document the chosen seam strategy in a test fixture docstring or CLAUDE.md entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `reads.py ensure_fresh` already degrades gracefully on fetch failure; if not, add to scope: specify the error-handling contract (exception swallowed + log warning vs. retry N times vs. circuit-breaker) and instrument with a log/metric so operators can observe fetch failures.
findings.finding: The fetch-failure AC ('A fetch failure yields consistent local state — no stall/corruption — in each facade') implies the production `reads.py ensure_fresh` already handles fetch errors with graceful degradation, but the plan does not confirm this. If new error-handling code must be written, there is no specification for retry/backoff/circuit-breaker behavior or how the error is surfaced to callers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A fetch failure yields consistent local state (no stall/corruption) in each facade'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the counted-fetch seam's full lifecycle: where it is created, what scope it covers (per-test-case, per-facade invocation), how it is reset, and whether writes are atomic when facades are exercised concurrently.
findings.finding: The 'counted fetch seam' is new shared/mutable state whose full lifecycle is unspecified: the plan does not say who creates the counter, how it is reset between individual test cases (or between facades within one test), or whether concurrent facade invocations in the same process will corrupt the count.
findings.location: Scope / Acceptance Criteria — 'Add a two-clone parity test with a counted fetch seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a thread-safe counter (e.g., `threading.Lock`-guarded int or `itertools.count` with atomic swap) injected per test case, and explicitly reset/re-inject it before each AC sub-assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the throttle marker lifecycle in the test: how it is created/seeded, how expiry is induced (e.g., backdating mtime or monkeypatching time), and that cleanup is guaranteed (e.g., via `tmp_path` fixture) even on test failure.
findings.finding: The throttle marker's lifecycle in the two-clone test context is unspecified: the plan does not say who creates the marker, how its expiry is simulated (time-travel vs. deletion), whether it is cleaned up after the test, or how isolation is ensured across test cases.
findings.location: Scope / Acceptance Criteria — 'throttle marker' and AC items 1, 2
findings.suggested_fix: Use pytest's `tmp_path` fixture for the marker file so it is automatically removed, and monkeypatch the time source used by `ensure_fresh` to control expiry deterministically without sleeping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit test assertion) that verifies all three facades call through the same `reads.ensure_fresh` code path, e.g., by asserting the counted-fetch seam is incremented exactly once across a combined CLI+library+MCP invocation sequence, or by inspecting call provenance.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria verify behavioral outcomes (visibility, fetch count) but do not include an observable proof that all three facades share a single implementation path — a passing test suite would be equally consistent with three independent implementations each accidentally satisfying the behavioral constraints, which would not satisfy the headline's 'shared throttle' guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all items; headline 'shared throttle'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'counted fetch seam' abstraction with the existing monkeypatch spy-lambda pattern (e.g. `seen = {}; def _spy(t, **kw): seen['n'] = seen.get('n',0)+1`) already used in `test_config_sync_aliases.py` — confirm no new reusable seam class/module is introduced unless it acquires ≥3 call-sites.
findings.finding: The plan names a 'counted fetch seam' as a new construct, but the codebase already achieves fetch-count assertions via plain monkeypatch.setattr on ensure_fresh (a spy lambda pattern used at two call-sites in test_config_sync_aliases.py). Introducing a named seam abstraction for a single test violates Rule-of-Three (zero existing call-sites for any such seam) and YAGNI (AC2 only requires a per-test counter, not a reusable instrumentation layer).
findings.location: Plan § What / Scope: 'a counted fetch seam'; AC line: 'Repeated reads within the throttle window produce exactly one fetch total.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new test, count fetches via: `calls = []; monkeypatch.setattr(reads, 'ensure_fresh', lambda *a, **kw: calls.append(1))` then assert `len(calls) == 1`. This reuses the established monkeypatch spy pattern (test_config_sync_aliases.py:167,191) with zero new abstraction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the cited test-file path from bare `test_no_stall.py` to `tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py` (verified location) so executors can find and reuse its fixtures.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'test_no_stall.py' as the existing test file covering the dispatcher-passthrough assertion, but no file by that exact name exists; the real file is tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py. This is a verifiable filename assertion that the repo refutes.
findings.location: Why section — '…a dispatcher-passthrough assertion that patches `ensure_fresh` (`tests/unit/test_config_sync_aliases.py`, `test_no_stall.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'test_no_stall.py' with 'tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py' in the Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's reference from bare `test_no_stall.py` to the actual path `tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py`.
findings.finding: The plan cites `test_no_stall.py` as an existing test that patches `ensure_fresh`, but no file with that name exists. The real file is `tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `test_no_stall.py` with `tests/interfaces/store/test_show_no_stall.py` in the Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to the 'What' or 'Scope' section explaining why a real two-clone git setup (rather than mocking `reconverge`) is necessary to prove the throttle-marker sharing and remote-visibility guarantees across facades.
findings.finding: The plan names the two-clone + counted-fetch-seam approach but does not state a positive rationale for WHY this approach fits over a purely mock-based test — the absence of even a sentence explaining the fitness (e.g. 'exercises the real git path so the throttle marker's filesystem behavior is proven, not mocked') leaves an implementer without a design anchor if they reconsider the approach mid-implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'What': 'A real bare-remote clone (not a mocked reconverge) is required because the guarantee under test — shared `/tmp/.ticket-sync-<md5>` marker prevents double-fetch across CLI/library/MCP call paths — is a filesystem-level property that a pure mock would not exercise.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that AC2's fetch-count assertion should use the established monkeypatch spy-lambda pattern (e.g. `calls=[]; monkeypatch.setattr(reads, 'ensure_fresh', lambda *a,**k: calls.append(1))`) rather than introducing a new named 'counted fetch seam' abstraction, unless the plan explicitly justifies ≥3 call-sites for the new construct.
findings.finding: The plan introduces 'a counted fetch seam' as a named new abstraction, but the codebase already solves fetch-count assertions with a plain monkeypatch spy-lambda (the `_fake_fresh` pattern at test_config_sync_aliases.py lines 160-167 and 184-191). Naming and building a formalized seam construct for a single test is an anti-repackaging of an already-established zero-infrastructure pattern, and violates Rule-of-Three (zero existing call-sites for any 'fetch seam' abstraction).
findings.location: Plan § What: 'a two-clone parity test with a counted fetch seam'; AC2: 'Repeated reads within the throttle window produce exactly one fetch total.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a counted fetch seam' in the What section with: 'a fetch-call spy (monkeypatch over ensure_fresh, consistent with the existing pattern in test_config_sync_aliases.py) to assert exactly one fetch within the throttle window'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
